posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
A friend served leftovers of this to me two years ago. It was a half bottle he’d commandeered after an in-store tasting two or three days earlier. The wine was spectacular – fresh, vibrant and structured – notwithstanding the air it had had. I bought a half case based on that showing.

I figured it was time to check in on this, though I was afraid it might be shut down. What a pleasant surprise! After I decanted it, I tasted the dregs, which had a small amount of sediment. Glorious! Fruity, structured. Pretty open.

When we sat down three hours after the decant, this paired extremely well with spaghetti and bolognese sauce, and got better and better. Very bright reddish fruit – slightly underripe red cherries or cranberries, I’d say. The friend I shared it with got something iodine-like, which I think might have been a trace bit of VA – just enough to give the wine a bit of lift. “Lifted” is just a great word for this. In the mouth, there is a lot of acidity, but there’s lots of bright cranberry-like fruit, too, and moderate tannin. In structure, this was more like a top premier cru Cotes de Nuits Burgundy than most Hermitage or Cornas. It’s about elegance, not power. Yet it still spoke of the Rhone.

This is just in adolescence. There is no tertiary evolution to speak of, and it doesn’t offer a great deal on the nose yet. I can’t really give it a masculine or feminine characterization (apart from political correctness concerns). It has so much finesse/grace, but it’s got muscles underneath. 94+ points from me.

Cote Rotie was my favorite wine for many years. Then I neglected it for a stretch and, over the past decade, I’ve had mostly disappointing experiences (Guigal, Jamet, Champet, Jasmin, Rostaing, Burgaud). This bottle is the real thing, and I’m sure it will be much better in 10 years. It reminded me of what I fell for in CR so many years ago.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: That sounds really nice. I think I had the same trajectory of Cote Rotie as you: starting out with it and then not buying much after the mid-late '90’s.

posts.name: Zachary Ross
posts.cooked: Great note. Levet is certainly among my very favorite Cote-Rotie domaines. Very glad to hear this bottle showed so well. I’d have expected it to be more closed and stern.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just bought a 1989 Levet! Never had any that old, and folks say they were brett bombs back then, but quite excited to try it nonetheless.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
That sounds really nice. I think I had the same trajectory of Cote Rotie as you: starting out with it and then not buying much after the mid-late '90’s.

Very much my story. I wasn’t buying much wine in the early 90s, then got interested in Barolo circa 1996 and focused there and forgot to buy Rhones when I started buying again. That was an oversight that I regret.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Just bought a 1989 Levet! Never had any that old, and folks say they were brett bombs back then, but quite excited to try it nonetheless.

I’m pretty insensitive to brett, but this seemed quite clean. (My friend, who is ITB, didn’t think there was VA, though I thought I was getting a faint hint of it.)

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note John. I have a bunch of this, and will dig one up this fall/winter.

posts.name: Eric Guido
posts.cooked: That sounds amazing. I only started collecting Levet with the 2011 and put a good stash away over the next few vintages. The only sad thing is that the word is officially out on them now, and as of 2015s, the wines were highly allocated. It seemed to happen over night.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: The 2010 La Chav is indeed spectacular, but of all the Levets I have tried (multiples of every vintage 04-15), this one could be the one for the longest haul. I love it, thought. And the 2010 Les Journaries is damn fine as well. Classic Cote Rotie.

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked: mmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmm, I love Levet and your note sums up why I love it. I just wish the frenzy Eric noted wasn’t as wide as it is
posts.display_username: Keith A k e r s

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: I had a 2011 Levet Charavorache a few days ago… decanted/cork put back in it for 8 hours or so… I enjoyed it (my favorite of the mix in similar price range). Wife didn’t. Said it smelled like socks.

I just cracked a 2005 earlier this year…and it still seemed like it had a good 3-5 years before hitting mid-maturity to me. I really enjoyed the 2005 and don’t think it would be a waste to open one now either.

suggested_topics.title: 2023 Chablis?
suggested_topics.excerpt:
It looks like some 2023 Chablis is starting to hit the shelves where I live. 
Has anyone had experience with this vintage? I wonder how it compares with the recent vintages.
